evidence.page_title: Looting After a Hurricane: It's Not About the Bread | HuffPost Life
evidence.title: Looting After a Hurricane: It's Not About the Bread
evidence.description: There are always a plethora of excuses and rationales for the looting after a natural disaster. If it's for bread, I get that. If it's for a flat screen TV or designer jeans or the latest sneakers, I don't.
evidence.text:
Hurricane Sandy has battered the East Coast. In New York City, 375,000 people were ordered to evacuate. Public schools are closed, the transit system shut down, businesses shuttered.
Sandy has been called a "super storm" because it has gobbled up other storm systems along the Atlantic seaboard, creating a storm swath visible from the space station. While many have made preparations to leave, I can only imagine some people are taking a different tact; they aren't preparing to leave, they're preparing to loot. With arguably millions of dollars of merchandise relatively unprotected (except for gale force winds, rain, flooding, tidal surges, etc.), some may conclude the time is ripe for a little shopping.
Looting has become part and parcel of large storms. I remember the stories that came out after Hurricane Katrina of people unabashedly looting stores right in front of National Guardsmen. I also remember seeing video of people wading out in thigh-high floodwaters with armloads of clothing and trashcans full of goods. The collective sentiment of the looters at the time appeared to be all that stuff was up for grabs because of the emergency. Sociologists call this a breakdown in the social order.
I wonder how much of a breakdown in the social order will occur over this latest storm. According to stories I saw just starting to percolate on some Internet sites, people were already tweeting their plans to loot. It wouldn't be the first time. Twitter appears to have been used to coordinate looting in England, even without the cover of a natural disaster. In the U.S., this activity of coordinated, multiple-participant theft is called flash mob robbery or flash rob for short. The tactic is to make sure enough people show up to overwhelm whatever security is on hand or to cause a limited, targeted breakdown in the social order -- long enough for you to take whatever you can.
Interestingly, such a breakdown in the social order did not occur after the Japanese tsunami in 2011. According to one story, people lined up in front of stores "patiently standing" for hours and "despite the line's length everyone remained calm and polite." The juxtaposition of one group of people standing quietly, calm and polite vs. another group of people wracking havoc and grabbing whatever they could is startling and, frankly, disturbing.
As concerning as it is to view the lawlessness and looting that sometimes occurs after a natural disaster, it is even more concerning to realize this kind of activity no longer requires a natural disaster; it merely requires enough people of like mind to connect via social media and decide to take what someone else has.
When I was growing up, I was taught it was wrong to take what didn't belong to me. Period. As I got older, I understood the dilemma of being hungry and stealing bread. But the vast majority of looting I've seen over the past decade since Katrina doesn't seem to me to be about bread. It's about using a breakdown in the social order for personal enrichment at the expense of others.
There are always a plethora of excuses and rationales for the looting after a natural disaster. If it's for bread, I get that. If it's for a flat screen TV or designer jeans or the latest sneakers, I don't. If you overlook a group of people trashing someone else's property because they can, what will you say if the next person they target is you?
Some may look askew at the calm and patiently-waiting Japanese, thinking them foolish for not taking advantage of an opportunity for some personal enrichment. I don't think so. When their society was stretched and stressed by a natural disaster, they didn't turn on each other, they turned to each other. When we don't find a way do the same, we create our own disaster.
For more by Dr. Gregory Jantz, Ph.D., click here.
For more on emotional intelligence, click here.

evidence.title: Wolf of Writing 205
evidence.description: Natural Disasters are unavoidable and catastrophic for the economy, location and people that are targeted.. So when a natural disaster does hit and affects people’s livelihood, how do we survive? D…
evidence.text:
Natural Disasters are unavoidable and catastrophic for the economy, location and people that are targeted.. So when a natural disaster does hit and affects people’s livelihood, how do we survive? Do we become selfless and help our neighbors, elders, and children or do we resort to animalistic behavior such as looting, killing and torment? Can this really be animalistic if we are just trying to survive? Do we deserve the same consequences, as we would receive prior to the disaster? Hurricane Katrina, of 2005, devastated New Orleans greater than any previous hurricane in the area, which caused many problems such as looting, violence and police corruption. This went along with Hurricane Sandy that hit last year in New Jersey, my home. This storm was also one of the most devastating hurricanes of that area. In both disasters, all classes experienced the difference between looting and surviving. My aunts, Jamie Rivera and Carey Backle, two residents and owners of coastal homes in New Jersey, talk about their experiences with looting, killing and surviving. Their experiences go along with former president, George W. Bush’s text about Katrina, and Spike Lee’s documentation during and after Hurricane Katrina hit New Orleans. This is significant because there is a great difference between surviving and looting and it needs to be recognized to prevent further decay of leadership, death, and destruction after any natural disaster.
During Hurricane Katrina and Hurricane Sandy, the affected areas were forced into a mandatory evacuation. The people that stayed in New Orleans were given the option of residing in the Dome, the football stadium where the New Orleans Saints play, to stay safe. However the promise of food and water was scarce and the amount of people was high.
George W. Bush, president at the time stated, “Eighty percent of the city, home to more than 450,000 people, had flooded. Reports of looting and violence filled the news.” Also that, “it was clear the authorities in Louisiana could not lead.” In this case is looting acceptable? First, it must be understood what looting is. It is the act of something appropriated illegally often by force or violence. Grocery stores were looted, food was taken as well as water, however, police force was still used to stop and kill any that were participating in these extricates. As a news reporter in Spike Lee’s documentary reporting on the devastation stated, “We are in a virtual refugee camp, with thousands of people waiting for some sort of help: medical, food, water… you name it… Over there the police, scores of police officers all concerned about one looter who’s in that super market.” If the people of New Orleans were nonviolent and just acquiring their needs to survive then they should not be punished but rather helped. When family members are starving and the government seems to be doing nothing, what other option do we as people have to be able to survive? Stealing water and food is much different than looting; it seems to be just a means of survival
While many politicians and news teams tried to paint the picture of poor people destroying grocery stores to get food as looting, this is simply survival. The actual looting was much worse. George W. Bush, recalled looters smashing windows, stealing guns, cloths and jewelry, gunfire to the point that helicopters could not land to help citizens stuck in their flooded homes, even buildings on fire. While many tried to blame this chaos on African Americans or poor people, my Aunt Carey Backle living in Toms River, New Jersey, an extremely affluent and affected area after Hurricane Sandy, recalls much of the same torment,
“There were people almost immediately after the storm coming into the town with metal detectors and trucks to fill. People’s homes that had already been destroyed by the hurricane were getting broken into and valued possessions such as jewelry and T.V.s were being stolen by neighbors.”
People became greedy and saw an opportunity in other’s suffering to gain materialistic possessions. The classic saying that money makes the world go around still was in affect during these detrimental times. With bodies lying on the streets and homes in wreckage, some peoples’ only reason for being around it was for selfish desires. Both New Orleans and New Jersey became more like a warzone than the country known to help all others in need.
In both hurricanes, police protection was obtained to block looters or rather potential looters from getting into other’s homes and businesses, however in many cases the police officers were part of the looting. Police officers are supposed to put themselves behind their citizens and help those in danger, but during Hurricane Katrina, George W. Bush evoked officers abandoning their posts, whether it was for their own personal reasons or to join in the pandemonium of looting. When a reporter asked a participating officer in New Orleans what she was doing, she simply responded, “I’m doing my job” all while filling a cart with electronics in Walmart. (Lee) Police corruption during natural disasters also occurred during Hurricane Sandy, years after viewing the failure in New Orleans. My Aunt Jamie Rivera, who owns a house on the bay recounts her experience with this police corruption,
“When I tried to go home to start restoring my house, I was greeted by a police blockade on my street to ‘prevent looting’. It took me and my husband almost an hour to prove our residence and go home. As we were doing this, we saw a police officer come out of a house with a T.V. I was shocked. After that I stayed in my house on a blowup mattress with no electricity to make sure that would not happen to me.”
The argument that police forces were only helping the victims of the storm was false. Tragedy brings out the worst in people who try to obtain and abuse their power and the vulnerable state of the victims.
Surviving and looting can be categorized differently depending on the situation, essentially dangerous looting was still prevalent in both Hurricane Katrina and Hurricane Sandy regardless of class. People turned on each other and became animalistic. There was no order from the government so people just ran rampant. Victims were killing other victims to get what they wanted rather than helping each other as my Aunt Carey saw. “My neighbor from down the street was killed, shot in the back of the head by a looter. This guy (the looter) posed as an electrician coming to fix the power and then shot him like he was nothing.” She goes further in talking about the item this person tried to steal was a hot water heater. Something that would not even work with the circumstances. People were losing their way and hurting each other rather than coming to a solution. While this was not true in all cases and a lot of people actually helped each other selflessly, deceased bodies still laid in the streets. As George W. Bush states, “Katrina conjures impressions of disorder, incompetence, and the sense that the government let down its citizens. Serious mistakes came at all levels…” For those that broke the law and stole and killed, there was no real consequence or justice. How could there be when people in the dome in New Orleans were dying left and right at the hands of their government? There is no reason for killing especially after such travesty already, but looting and the crowd mentality of violence reined supreme in peoples’ minds.
Human natural tells us that to survive we will most likely do whatever it takes, such as stealing, looting and even killing, but does that make it right? In any disaster there will be those that become outstanding selfless individuals that would do whatever they can to help, and those that will use that vulnerable state to reach their own selfish fantasies. It does not matter if a person is black, white, has a badge or not people will still loot and kill. In both Hurricane Katrina and Hurricane Sandy it has been discussed the multiple types of looting and the effects of this crime. People who steal DVDs and electronics or other peoples’ belongings are one type of survivor while those that break into a grocery store for food, water and clothes is another. It depends on your own viewpoint, but it should be noted that these are two different things and should not be categorized together, just like Jamie Rivera and the man that shot Carey Backle’s neighbor are not the same type of gun owners. Natural disasters bring out the worst and the best in people and the way they survive. What would you do if your mother, child, brother were dying of dehydration? Would you become a looter, a victim of your government, or a survivor?
